title: Putin says he thinks Russia-Ukraine war is coming to an end
description: Russian President Vladimir Putin said on Saturday that he thought the Ukraine war was coming to an end, remarks that came just hours after he had vowed victory in Ukraine at ...
fullText:
MOSCOW, May 9 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin said on Saturday that he thought the Ukraine war was coming to an end, remarks that came just hours after he had vowed victory in Ukraine at Moscow's most scaled-back Victory Day parade in years.

"I think that ‚Äåthe matter is coming to an end," Putin told reporters of the Russia-Ukraine war, Europe's deadliest conflict since World War Two. He also said he would ‚Äåbe willing to negotiate new security arrangements for Europe, and that his preferred negotiating partner would be Germany's former Chancellor Gerhard Schroeder.

Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine triggered the most serious crisis in relations between Russia and the ‚ÄãWest since the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, when many people feared the world was on the brink of nuclear war.

The Kremlin has said peace talks brokered by U.S. President Donald Trump's administration were on pause. Putin has repeatedly vowed to fight on until all of Russia's various war aims are achieved in what Moscow calls the "special military operation".

Putin was speaking in the Kremlin after setting out his view of the causes of the war. He blamed "globalist" Western leaders, saying they promised NATO would not expand eastward after the 1989 fall of the Berlin Wall, but ‚Äåthen tried to draw Ukraine into the European Union's orbit.

His statement ‚ÅÝcame just hours after the parade on the May 9 national holiday celebrating the Soviet Union's victory over Nazi Germany in World War Two. The annual event pays homage to the 27 million Soviet citizens who perished in that war.

Instead of the usual intercontinental ballistic missiles, tanks ‚ÅÝand missile systems rolling across the cobbles of Red Square, Russia played a video of its military hardware in action on giant screens opposite the Kremlin walls.

Russian troops have been fighting in Ukraine for well over four years. That is longer than Soviet forces fought in World War Two, known in Russia as the Great Patriotic War of 1941-45.

Putin, who has ruled Russia as president ‚Äãor ‚Äãprime minister since the last day of 1999, faces a wave of anxiety in Moscow about the ‚Äãwar in Ukraine, which has killed hundreds of thousands of people, ‚Äåleft swathes of Ukraine in ruins, and drained Russia's $3 trillion economy. Russia's relations with Europe are worse than at any time since the depths of the Cold War.

Russian forces have so far been unable to take the whole of the Donbas region of eastern Ukraine where Kyiv's forces have been pushed back to a line of fortress cities. Russian advances have slowed this year, though Moscow controls just under one fifth of Ukrainian territory.

After Russia and Ukraine accused each other of violating unilateral ceasefires they had each declared over recent days, U.S. President Donald Trump announced a three-day ceasefire from Saturday to Monday that was supported by the Kremlin and Kyiv. The two sides also agreed to exchange 1,000 prisoners.

"I'd like ‚Äåto see it stop. Russia-Ukraine - it's the worst thing since World War Two in terms of life. ‚ÄãTwenty-five thousand young soldiers every month. It's crazy," Trump told reporters in Washington.

He added that he would "like to ‚Äãsee a big extension" of the ceasefire. There were no reports of violations ‚Äãof the ceasefire from either Moscow or Kyiv.

European Council President Antonio Costa said last week he believed there was "potential" for the EU ‚Äåto negotiate with Russia, and to discuss the future of the ‚Äãsecurity architecture of Europe.

Asked if he was willing ‚Äãto engage in talks with the Europeans, Putin said the preferable figure for him was Schroeder.

"For me personally, the former Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany, Mr. Schroeder, is preferable," Putin said.

European leaders have said Russia must be defeated in Ukraine and cast Putin as a war criminal and autocrat who they say could ‚Äãone day attack a NATO member if he is allowed to ‚Äåwin the war. Russia dismisses such claims as nonsense.

Putin, who ordered troops into Ukraine in February 2022, casts European powers as warmongers for supporting Ukraine ‚Äãwith tens of billions of dollars in support, weapons and intelligence.

Asked about Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, Putin said a meeting was possible only once a ‚Äãlasting peace deal was agreed.

(Writing by Guy Faulconbridge and Vladimir Soldatkin; Editing by David Gregorio)

title: As economic despair mounts, Russian official admits the country has had enough of Putin‚Äôs war on Ukraine. ‚ÄòWe can‚Äôt even take one region‚Äô
description: ‚ÄúThe overall mood is that‚Äôs enough already; you‚Äôve been fighting for long enough.‚Äù
fullText:
Vladimir Putin is losing the Russian people as the economy and his war machine go in reverse amid withering Ukrainian attacks.

On the economic front, Putin himself recently revealed that GDP contracted in the first two months of the year. And on the Ukraine front, Russian forces suffered a net loss of territory last month for the first time since 2024.

After Russia launched a sudden invasion in 2022, Putin has not only failed to defeat Ukraine, his forces have been unable to take full control of the Donetsk region.

‚ÄúThe overall mood is that‚Äôs enough already; you‚Äôve been fighting for long enough,‚Äù a Russian official told the Washington Post last week on condition of anonymity. ‚ÄúIt seems to everyone that it‚Äôs been going on for longer than World War II, the Great Patriotic War ‚Äî and at the same time we can‚Äôt even take one region.‚Äù

With Western military aid and innovations from Ukraine‚Äôs now-thriving domestic defense industry, Kyiv has weakened Russia‚Äôs economy and military.

Long-range drone strikes deep into Russian territory have damaged key oil-export hubs and ‚Äúshadow fleet‚Äù tankers transporting sanctioned crude.

At the same time, new drone technology is also giving Ukraine a battlefield advantage, helping to roll back Russian troops, who have also been cut off from Starlink internet connections that were vital to their own drones.

In a tacit acknowledgement of the heightened threat from Ukraine‚Äôs drones, the Kremlin said Wednesday it would dramatically scale back the annual Victory Day parade in Moscow‚Äôs Red Square later this month.

Meanwhile, ordinary Russians have grappled with high inflation caused by military mobilizations and defense production as well as the Kremlin‚Äôs crackdown on internet access to restrict the flow of grim news on the economy and war.

Even a survey from Russia‚Äôs state-owned pollster showed Putin‚Äôs approval rate has fallen to 65.6% from 77.8% at the start of the year and prewar levels well above 80%.

Recognizing the growing economic despair, some Russian officials have publicly addressed the dire situation.

Last month, Economy Development Minister Maxim Reshetnikov told a business conference that the economy ‚Äúis not easy‚Äù and called for reallocating the workforce, which has been tight as the war created labor shortages.

‚ÄúOf course, it‚Äôs not easy to find staff, and salaries are rising,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúBut nonetheless, we coped with all of that somehow because somewhere in the economy there were reserves. Our current records show that these reserves have largely been used up; this truly is the situation and the macroeconomic situation is substantially more difficult.‚Äù

title: Putin praises Iranian people for resistance to US in talks with Araqchi
description: MOSCOW, April 27 () - Russian President Vladimir Putin on Monday praised the Iranian people for battling to stay independent in the face of U. and Israeli ‚Äåpressure and said Moscow would do all it cou...
fullText: MOSCOW, April 27 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin on Monday praised the Iranian people for battling to stay independent in the face of U.S. and Israeli ‚Äåpressure and said Moscow would do all it could to help Tehran. Russia ‚Äåhas offered to mediate to try to help restore calm to the Middle East following U.S. and Israeli ‚Äãstrikes, which Moscow has strongly condemned. It has also repeatedly offered to store Iran's enriched uranium as a way of defusing tensions, a proposal spurned by the United States. "We see how courageously and heroically the Iranian people are fighting for their independence and sovereignty," Putin told Araqchi, saying ‚Äåhe hoped they could get ‚ÅÝthrough what he called a "difficult period" and that peace would prevail. "For our part, we will do everything that serves your interests and the interests ‚ÅÝof all the peoples of the region to ensure that peace is achieved as quickly as possible," said Putin. Putin received Araqchi in the presidential library in Russia's former imperial capital St ‚ÄãPetersburg as ‚Äãsources from mediator Pakistan said work had not ‚Äãhalted to bridge gaps between the ‚ÄåUnited States and Iran. Putin said he received a message from Iran‚Äôs new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, last week, and asked Araqchi to convey to him that Russia intended to continue its strategic partnership with Tehran. That 20-year agreement was sealed last year. Russia is building two new nuclear units at Bushehr - the site of Iran's only nuclear power plant - ‚Äåand Iran has supplied Russia with Shahed drones for ‚Äãuse against Ukraine, the production of which Moscow has ‚Äãsince localised. Araqchi, who said he ‚Äãwanted to brief Putin on the situation around his country, thanked Putin ‚Äåfor Moscow's support. "It has also been proven ‚Äãto all that Iran ‚Äãhas friends and allies, such as the Russian Federation, who stand by Iran precisely in difficult times," he said. Dmitry Peskov, Putin's spokesman, said that Moscow wanted to ‚Äãsee the U.S. and Iran ‚Äåcontinue negotiations. There should be no return to military action, he added, something ‚Äãhe said was not in anyone's interests.

title: Major blow to Putin in Africa as Russian forces driven from Mali stronghold by separatists, jihadists
description: Analysts say Russia's Africa Corps defeat in Mali could be a turning point for Moscow's influence in West Africa as jihadist attacks continue.
fullText:
JOHANNESBURG: A major defeat for Russia‚Äôs mercenary Africa Corps, in a battle with jihadist and tribal militia, is being reported in Mali. Deadly attacks by al Qaeda-linked rebels and mostly-Muslim Tuareg tribesmen across cities in the West African country are continuing Monday, also with reports of senior figures in Mali‚Äôs military junta being killed or injured.

Analysts say this could be a turning point in Moscow‚Äôs influence in West Africa. Russia has been grabbing Mali‚Äôs precious minerals, including gold, in return for promising to protect the country against the rebels. Sunday, though, rebels reported that Russian mercenaries have been forced to retreat from their base in the northern city of Kidal.

"The attacks are a major blow to Russia", Ulf Laessing, head of the Sahel program at the Konrad Adenauer Foundation, told the Associated Press. He added, "The (Russian) mercenaries had no intelligence about the attacks and were unable to protect major cities. They have unnecessarily worsened the conflict by not distinguishing between civilians and combatants."

Video of dead soldiers who appear with their features to be Russian, and of Russian military vehicles reportedly leaving Kidal, has been seen by Fox News Digital.

Us Allowing Some Nigeria Embassy Staff To Evacuate Over 'Deteriorating Security Situation'

The Defense News Nigeria media site reported that Russia‚Äôs Africa Corps stated in Kidal "they were heavily outnumbered by a ratio of 6 to 1," adding "the Corps claimed that between 10,000 and 12,000 fighters were involved in yesterday‚Äôs attacks. There are 2,000 Russian mercenaries operating in Mali. The Malian government pays Wagner $10 million per month for security," according to the Nigerian publication.

A Russian blog on Telegram Monday, claiming to come from Kidal, stated "the units of the African Corps that were stationed and fought in Kidal left this settlement together with the military personnel of the Malian army. First of all, wounded servicemen and heavy equipment were evacuated. The personnel continues to carry out the assigned combat mission. The situation in the Republic of Mali remains difficult."

A spokesperson for the Tuareg-led Azawad Liberation Front, or FLA, told the Associated Press that the Russian troops have withdrawn from the city after a "white" agreement was reached. This has not been confirmed by either Mali‚Äôs government or Russia‚Äôs Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

Trump's Approach To Africa Lauded By Top Republican As Recent Airstrikes Show 'Outside The Box' Thinking

Russia's ministry of foreign affairs said on its Telegram account that "two hundred and fifty militants attacked¬ÝBamako Senou International Airport and the military base located nearby. The Malian Armed Forces repelled the attack and are currently taking further steps¬Ýto eliminate the militia¬Ýthat may have been, reportedly,¬Ýtrained by Western security agencies. Russia is deeply concerned about these developments. This terrorist activity poses a direct threat to¬Ýthe stability of friendly Mali¬Ýand could have the most serious consequences for the entire region."

Mali‚Äôs military junta, which first took power in a coup in 2020, Monday announced that both the chief of the armed forces‚Äô general staff, Gen. Oumar Diarra, and the director of the national security agency, Gen. Modibo Kon√©, have been wounded in attacks.

The government confirmed earlier that Sadio Camara, said to be the second most important figure in Mali‚Äôs military junta, had been killed in the fighting. Camara was Mali‚Äôs defense minister, and is widely credited with bringing in Moscow‚Äôs mercenary Africa Corps to assist in battling the militia.

The rebels attempting to take over Mali are said to be jihadists from the Nusrat al-Islam (GSIM/JNIM) linked with the tribesmen of the FLA and other groups, including at least one linked with Islamic State. The GSIM‚Äôs declared aim is to turn Mali into an al Qaeda-linked Islamic caliphate with strict Sharia law.

"The United States strongly condemns the terrorist attack in Mali," a State Department spokesperson told Fox News Digital Monday, adding: "We extend our deepest condolences to the victims, their families, and all those affected. We stand with the Malian people and government in the face of this violence. The United States remains committed to supporting efforts to advance peace, stability, and security across Mali and the region."

The U.S. Embassy in Mali warned Sunday on its website that "U.S. citizens should continue to shelter in place, remain alert, follow local news for updates, and avoid areas where security operations may be underway."

On Monday, multiple reports said Malian troops and Russian mercenaries have also been forced by rebels to withdraw from the northern Malian city of Tessit. At the time of publication, this had not been confirmed by the Malian government.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

Original article

title: Russia is facing a spring of discontent ‚Äì and Putin is responding the only way he knows how
description: Four years after the full-scale invasion of Ukraine, Russia is facing a spring of discontent.
fullText:
Four years after the full-scale invasion of Ukraine, Russia is facing a spring of discontent.

Rolling digital blackouts in Russian cities have touched a nerve with ordinary citizens and public pushback against Russian President Vladimir Putin is emerging.

Russia has weathered wartime economic pain while its security services keep protests in check. And the conflict in the Middle East has given Russia‚Äôs war effort an unexpected boost through higher oil prices.

Nonetheless, Russia‚Äôs repressive apparatus of state now appears to be shifting into high gear. In recent weeks, law-enforcement authorities have launched a new round of high-profile political arrests and raids. And in parallel, the Russian government has been resurrecting the ghosts of the Soviet past.

The most recent example: On Tuesday, officials from Russia‚Äôs Investigative Committee raided the offices of one of Russia‚Äôs largest publishers and detained staff members, following a year-old criminal investigation into what authorities allege is a case of ‚ÄúLGBTQ propaganda.‚Äù

The publisher, Eksmo, is the owner of an imprint called Popcorn Books that published young-adult fiction.

One of its titles appears to have drawn particular scrutiny: ‚ÄúSummer in a Pioneer Tie,‚Äù a 2021 bestseller featuring the story of a queer romance between two young men at a Soviet summer camp.

Authorities detained several individuals connected to the publishing house last year; the Popcorn Books imprint was shut down in January.

Putin‚Äôs Russia has long been hostile to what it deems to be dangerous Western ideas, with the Kremlin leader positioning himself as a defender of traditional values.

In 2023, Russia‚Äôs Supreme Court declared what Russian authorities call the ‚Äúinternational LGBTQ movement‚Äù an extremist organization, imposing potentially serious criminal penalties for LGBTQ activism ‚Äì or apparently, in the case of Eksmo, the act of publishing.

Russian state news agency TASS said top Eksmo managers were released on bail after questioning. But the publishing industry is not the only place where space for free expression is dwindling in Russia.

Earlier this month, police raided the offices of Novaya Gazeta, the independent newspaper whose co-founder won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2021.

Russian state news agency RIA-Novosti, citing the Ministry of Internal Affairs, said journalist Oleg Roldugin was detained for questioning in connection with a criminal case over the alleged illegal mishandling of personal data Roldugin denied guilt ahead of a hearing.

The chilling effect of the case is clear.

Novaya Gazeta was forced to shut down its print edition after the 2022 invasion of Ukraine but continues to publish online; the raid pushes the remnants of Russia‚Äôs free press further to the margins.

Sharing independent news in Russia is already tough. The government bans popular social media platforms such as Facebook and Instagram and is pushing to impose a state-controlled messaging app called MAX as the population‚Äôs default portal for digital services. And the Novaya Gazeta raid came on the same day as Russia‚Äôs Supreme Court designated Memorial, the storied human-rights organization, as ‚Äúextremist.‚Äù

In a statement, UN human rights chief Volker T√ºrk said the designation was ‚Äúeffectively criminalizing critical human rights work‚Äù in Russia.

While the attack on the press is underway, the authorities are also reviving old symbols of political repression. A few days ago Russia‚Äôs FSB Academy, where Putin trained to be a KGB agent, was renamed in honor of Feliks Dzherzinsky, the dreaded founder of the Soviet secret police.

The toppling of Dzherzinsky‚Äôs statue outside KGB headquarters in 1991 was one of the symbolic acts that marked the end of the Soviet Union. But authorities in Russia appear intent on embracing the country‚Äôs dark, totalitarian past.

On Thursday, Reuters reported, the embassies of Poland, Estonia, Lithuania and Latvia issued a protest to the Russian Foreign Ministry, after a memorial complex in the Siberian city of Tomsk dedicated to the victims of the Soviet secret police was dismantled. And earlier this month, Russia prompted outrage by installing an exhibit that some commentators said defiled the Katyn Memorial, the site of the mass execution of Polish POWs by the Soviets in 1940.

But if the Russian government is resurrecting the ghosts of the Soviet past ‚Äì and making life for ordinary Russians a whole lot more inconvenient ‚Äì Putin himself is showing public indifference.

On Thursday, Putin broke his silence on the rolling digital blackouts, which hit the country‚Äôs capital in early March.

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt help but point out what people are also encountering in large cities ‚Äî it‚Äôs rare, but unfortunately, it does happen,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm referring to certain internet problems and outages in major metropolitan areas.‚Äù

Putin said the unpopular internet disruptions ‚Äì which have hit e-commerce and made many apps and electronic services inaccessible ‚Äì were ‚Äúrelated to operational work to prevent terrorist attacks.‚Äù But he also appeared to suggest that the public‚Äôs need to know was limited.

‚ÄúWidespread public information in advance can be detrimental to operational development, because the criminals, after all, hear and see everything,‚Äù he said ‚ÄúAnd, of course, if information reaches them, they will adjust their criminal behavior and their criminal plans.‚Äù

In other words, life during wartime means putting up with some inconvenience. And Russia‚Äôs security services broadening and deepening a crackdown on civic life shows little sign of abating.

title: Trump says he‚Äôs having ‚Äúgood conversations‚Äù with Putin and Zelenskiy on Ukraine
description: Trump says he‚Äôs having ‚Äúgood conversations‚Äù with Putin and Zelenskiy on Ukraine
fullText:
Investing.com --¬ÝU.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday he was optimistic about efforts to end the war in Ukraine, telling Fox News he has been in regular contact with Russian President Vladimir Putin and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy.

‚ÄúWe‚Äôre working on the Russia-Ukraine situation, and hopefully we‚Äôre going to get it,‚Äù Trump said in an interview on¬ÝFox News‚Äô ‚ÄúThe Sunday Briefing.‚Äù

He declined to give details on his most recent call with Putin but said he has had ‚Äúgood conversations‚Äù with both leaders.

Trump said personal animosity between Putin and Zelenskiy was complicating efforts to reach a peace deal. ‚ÄúThe hatred between President Putin and President Zelenskiy is ridiculous. It‚Äôs crazy,‚Äù he said.

Trump had pledged to bring the nearly three-year-old conflict to a swift end, and while peace efforts have intensified, including direct Ukraine-Russia talks and a 20-point peace framework that is reported to be 90% agreed upon - key sticking points remain, with neither Ukraine nor Russia having yet accepted a final proposal.

‚ÄúAnd hate is a bad thing. Hate is ‚Äãa bad thing when you‚Äôre trying to settle something, but it‚Äôll happen,‚Äù Trump said.

title: Putin's approval rating falls to lowest since before Ukraine war, state pollster says
description: MOSCOW, April 24 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin's approval rating has fallen for a seventh week in a row to 65.6%, its lowest level since the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022,
fullText:
MOSCOW, April 24 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin's approval rating has fallen for a seventh week in a row to 65.6%, its lowest level since the invasion of Ukraine ‚Äåin February 2022, according to state pollster VTsIOM.

Russia's paramount leader, who was appointed acting president ‚Äåby Boris Yeltsin just eight years after the fall of the Soviet Union, has ruled the country since 1999 either as ‚Äãpresident or prime minister.

A former KGB lieutenant colonel who watched the Soviet Union collapse while based in East Germany, Putin will overtake Josef Stalin and become Russia's longest-serving ruler since Empress Catherine the Great if he completes his current six-year term.

But his ratings, though still high by Western standards, have taken a hit ‚Äåin recent weeks. Putin's approval rating ‚ÅÝis down from 73.3% in March, while trust in him has slipped over the same period to 71% from above 77%, according to VTsIOM.

It was not immediately ‚ÅÝclear why Putin's rating, though still very high, was falling. Last week he ordered his top officials to come up with ways to kick-start the economy after a contraction in the first two months of the year.

A ‚Äãcrackdown ‚Äãon the mobile internet, messengers and VPNs has caused ‚Äãfrustration for many Russians in the past ‚Äåfew months.

Putin said on Thursday that the internet outages were necessary for security reasons, but that law enforcement officials must show "ingenuity" to find solutions and guarantee the functioning of vital services.

Amid war and state censorship, the question of what Russians truly think is the subject of considerable debate. Putin's supporters point to polls showing widespread approval of his rule but opponents question how truthful people are when speaking ‚Äåto pollsters in such a controlled environment.

His approval rating jumped ‚Äãafter the invasion of Ukraine, rising to just below 80% ‚Äãfrom 64.3%, and has stayed well over ‚Äã75% for most of the war, albeit with brief dips after mobilisation to ‚Äåthe army was announced in 2022, according to ‚ÄãVTsIOM.

Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin's trust ‚Äãrating fell to 53.8%, former president Dmitry Medvedev's rose to 36.8%, Communist leader Gennady Zyuganov's rating rose to 32.7% and Just Russia leader Sergei Mironov's rating rose to 29.8%.

Ahead of a ‚Äãparliamentary election due by late ‚ÄåSeptember, there have been a number of unusually public warnings by some bloggers and politicians ‚Äãthat there must be change in Russia to avoid the risk of unrest.

title: Another wave of public outcry tests Putin's rule in wartime Russia
description: While none of this dissent indicates an imminent threat to Putin‚Äôs rule, analysts say it presents a new and growing challenge for the Kremlin.  ‚ÄúGreater and greater effort needs to be spent on maintai...
fullText:
Some Russian influencers recently released public appeals to President Vladimir Putin, criticizing his government and policies, and a number of his loyalists even threatened a revolt ‚Äî the latest wave of public outcry over the country's strained wartime economy and mounting internet restrictions.

While none of this dissent indicates an imminent threat to Putin‚Äôs rule, analysts say it presents a new and growing challenge for the Kremlin.

‚ÄúGreater and greater effort needs to be spent on maintaining the status quo,‚Äù Mark Galeotti, an expert on Russian politics who heads the Mayak Intelligence consultancy, wrote in an analysis.

Here‚Äôs a look at the public outcry in Russia and what is driving it:

A 19-minute video by popular Russian blogger Victoria Bonya has received 31 million views on Instagram since it was published 10 days ago.

In the video, Bonya, who has 13.6 million followers on the platform, complained to Putin that he was probably misinformed about some things ‚Äî local authorities' poor handling of recent floods in the southern province of Dagestan, the culling of livestock in Siberia that prompted protests from farmers, crippling internet restrictions and strains on small businesses.

Bonya, a popular Russian TV host who now lives abroad, emphasized that she supports Putin, but she said ordinary Russians and his own officials are too scared to tell him the truth.

‚ÄúThere‚Äôs a lot you don‚Äôt know,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúPeople are screaming at the top of their lungs now. They‚Äôve been robbed of everything they have, and they continue to be robbed. Businesses are dying.‚Äù

Reactions to the video snowballed. Other Russian influencers aired similar sentiments in their videos, some of which were later deleted.

In a rare acknowledgment of the public criticism, Putin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said Kremlin officials saw the video and that ‚Äúa lot of work is being done‚Äù on the issues Bonya mentioned. ‚ÄúNone of it is being ignored,‚Äù Peskov said.

Communist Party leader Gennady Zyuganov, a longtime Putin supporter, lambasted the government Tuesday in a speech to parliament, saying that his party had raised the issues before. He threatened a repeat of the 1917 Bolshevik Revolution if measures are not taken to deal with the problems.

Forecasts of a revolt also have been regularly floated in pro-Kremlin Telegram channels and by loyal military bloggers.

In the meantime, Russian state-controlled pollster VTsIOM has reported a consistent decline of Putin‚Äôs approval ratings in recent weeks. Data released Friday showed his approval at 65.6%, the lowest level the pollster has reported since before the war in Ukraine, down from the 77.8% in late December 2025.

Russia's top independent pollster, the Levada Center, also reported a slight decline in Putin's approvals, from 85% in October 2025 to 80% in March.

Russians across the vast country have faced regular cellphone internet shutdowns since last spring. The authorities have justified them as a way to thwart Ukrainian drone attacks, but critics have argued that the outages are another step in a yearslong effort to bring the internet under tight government control.

The shutdowns came on top of sweeping, ever-growing internet censorship that over the years saw thousands of websites and platforms in Russia blocked or throttled, including the two most popular messaging apps ‚Äî WhatsApp and Telegram.

Authorities are promoting a new state-backed messaging app, Max, seen by many as a surveillance tool, while also blocking VPNs to stop widespread censorship circumvention.

Public frustration over the measures elicited acts of resistance, including petitions to the presidential administration, a class-action lawsuit against the government, a few street pickets and multiple attempts to organize bigger protests that were quashed by authorities.

The Kremlin appears unfazed. At a government meeting Thursday, Putin again justified the shutdowns as necessary to ‚Äúprevent terror attacks‚Äù and urged authorities to better inform the public about restrictions.

His remarks indicate that the security services ‚Äúare doing everything correctly, and it will continue for as long as they see fit," Tatiana Stanovaya of the Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center wrote in a Telegram post.

The critical videos have emerged at a time of growing strain on the country‚Äôs wartime economy.

Economic growth stopped after the initial boost from massive military spending wore off. High interest rates imposed by the central bank to control inflation and increased taxes have also weighed on businesses.

Economic Minister Maxim Reshetnikov said recently that the economy's reserves ‚Äúhave been largely depleted,‚Äù and Putin said at a televised government meeting earlier this month that economic growth has declined for two months in a row. Russia's gross domestic product shrank by 1.8% between January and February, he said.

Denis Volkov, director of the Levada Center, said economic problems are the main driver of the growing dissatisfaction and declining approval for Putin and the government.

‚ÄúIt begins to show in the opinion polls, when the mood starts to get worse, just because life becomes harder,‚Äù Volkov said.

Sam Greene, professor of Russian politics at King's College London, also points to dwindling hopes that Russia‚Äôs war in Ukraine, now in its fifth year, could soon end.

Those hopes coalesced after U.S. President Donald Trump took office in January 2025 and spearheaded an effort to negotiate a peace deal that has since stalled.

‚ÄúThe Kremlin was really putting some weight behind that idea as well. And I think that became priced into public opinion,‚Äù Greene said. ‚ÄúAnd yet that‚Äôs not happening."

The resulting disappointment and frustration means that Putin ‚Äúpays a bit of a price.‚Äù

Galeotti said in his analysis that ‚Äúnone of this can be taken to herald the imminent end of Putin‚Äôs rule.‚Äù

There is ‚Äúno meaningful organized opposition,‚Äù and Putin‚Äôs ‚Äúcontrol of the security apparatus is unchallenged," Galeotti said. In a war, "even his critics do not want to destabilize the country.‚Äù

Volkov echoed that thinking and said the discontent grows only slowly. Putin‚Äôs approvals are declining ‚Äúfrom a very high point.‚Äù

‚ÄúFor now, we shouldn‚Äôt downplay or exaggerate this, because we‚Äôre only at the very beginning of the road,‚Äù he said.

In the meantime, frustration will continue to deepen, with people feeling empowered by popular public figures voicing criticism, said Abbas Gallyamov, a former Putin speechwriter turned political analyst.

‚ÄúThe feeling of power in politics," he said, "is largely tied to how widespread the position that you share and defend is.‚Äù

title: Ukraine has asked Turkey to host a Zelenskiy-Putin meeting, FM says
description: KYIV, April 22 (Reuters) - Ukraine has asked Turkey to host a meeting between President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and Russian President Vladimir Putin, its top diplomat said, as Kyiv seeks to reinvigorate
fullText:
KYIV, April 22 (Reuters) - Ukraine has asked Turkey to host a meeting between President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Äåand Russian President Vladimir Putin, its top diplomat ‚Äåsaid, as Kyiv seeks to reinvigorate stalling peace talks.

"We asked the ‚ÄãTurks about it, we asked some other capitals," Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha said in comments to reporters on Tuesday that were cleared for release on Wednesday.

He added that ‚ÄåUkraine would be ‚ÅÝready to consider any place other than Belarus or Russia for a meeting with Putin, ‚ÅÝwhich Zelenskiy has long sought to try to hasten a resolution of the more than four-year war.

Belarus is ‚Äãa close ‚Äãally of Russia and ‚Äãallowed Moscow to use ‚ÄåBelarusian territory to launch its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

Sybiha did not say how Ankara had responded to the proposal.

"We addressed the Turks specifically," he said. "But if another capital, besides Moscow and Belarus, organises such a ‚Äåmeeting, we will go."

The Kremlin ‚Äãpreviously said it is willing ‚Äãto host Zelenskiy in ‚ÄãMoscow, where the Ukrainian leader has said ‚Äåhe will not go.

Separately, Sybiha ‚Äãsaid that ‚Äãhe had already exchanged written messages with Anita Orban, who will become Hungary's new foreign minister when ‚Äãthe new government, ‚Äåwhich won the election there earlier this month, ‚Äãtakes power.

title: Kremlin says Putin can meet Zelenskiy only to agree final conflict arrangements
description: April 22 () - Russian President Vladimir Putin can only meet Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy for the purpose of finalising agreements on ‚Äåthe conflict, Russian news agencies quoted Kremlin spo...
fullText:
April 22 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin can only meet Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy for the purpose of finalising agreements on ‚Äåthe conflict, Russian news agencies quoted Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov as ‚Äåsaying on Wednesday.

Peskov's comments on state television, reported by Russian news agencies, came after Ukraine's ‚ÄãForeign Minister, Andrii Sybiha said Ukraine asked Turkey to host a meeting between between the two leaders to reinvigorate stalling peace talks.

"The main thing is the goal of this meeting. Why should they meet? Putin has said ‚Äåhe is ready for a ‚ÅÝmeeting in Moscow at any moment," the news agencies quoted Peskov as saying.

"The main thing is that there should ‚ÅÝbe a reason to meet, and the main thing is that the meeting should be productive. And it can only be for the purpose of finalising agreements."

Peskov ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãany such meeting required careful preparation and "political ‚Äãwill from the Kyiv regime, ‚ÄåAnd for now, we see no such political will."

He said Russia's main aim in its military drive, known as a "special military operation" was to ensure its vital interests and guard against Ukrainian attacks on energy and other targets which he described as "the danger of terrorism from the Kyiv region".

He ‚Äåsaid Russia was ready for any new ‚Äãtalks on a settlement to the war ‚Äãwith U.S. negotiators "even tomorrow".

In his ‚Äãearlier comments, Sybiha said Kyiv had asked Turkey to ‚Äåhost a meeting between the two ‚Äãleaders but did not ‚Äãsay how Ankar had responded to the request.

The Turkish presidency said President Tayyip Erdogan told NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte in a ‚Äãmeeting that Ankara was ‚Äåmaking efforts to revive negotiations between Russia and Ukraine and ‚Äãbring together the leaders of the warring sides.

title: Russian blogger whose angry appeal to Putin went viral hits back after state TV attack
description: A celebrity blogger whose sharp criticism of the authorities has been watched by tens of millions of Russians has hit back after one of state TV's best-
fullText:
MOSCOW, April 20 (Reuters) - A celebrity blogger whose sharp criticism of the authorities has been watched by tens of millions of Russians has hit back after one of state TV's best-known political presenters accused her of working for the ‚ÄåWest and called for her to be investigated.

Vladimir Solovyov, a fiery talk show host sanctioned by the West over his vocal ‚Äåsupport for the war in Ukraine, launched a vitriolic weekend attack on Victoria Bonya live on air, mixing personal insults about her appearance with accusations that she is part ‚Äãof a shadowy Western plot to undermine the Kremlin.

Bonya, who lives in Monaco and has denied working for anyone but herself, called Solovyov "an enemy of the people" who should be taken off air. She said he was one of several state TV personalities who spoke about women in an unacceptable way.

"I want to ask a question to all of us women - when did we miss the moment when women began to be insulted on federal TV channels?" ‚Äåsaid Bonya, a 46-year-old single mother, adding she was ‚ÅÝtired of women like herself being publicly dismissed as prostitutes or escorts.

"There are lots of mothers who bring up their kids on their own. By insulting me you insult them all," said Bonya, pointing out that her ‚ÅÝpage had received nearly 500 million visits and that her social media presence was bigger than Solovyov's.

Bonya, well-known in Russia as a former reality TV star, had hitherto confined herself to videos about her own life and beauty tips, garnering over 13 million Instagram followers.

But she triggered a political storm with a video appeal ‚Äãto ‚ÄãPresident Vladimir Putin saying she supported him but that he was not being ‚Äãtold the truth about Russia's real problems, and that ordinary ‚Äåpeople were being squeezed so hard by corrupt officials that they might one day erupt.

The Kremlin took the unusual step of acknowledging Bonya's criticism - her video clip has since been viewed nearly 30 million times, - while insisting that the problems she listed - including a sweeping crackdown on the internet - were being worked on. It denied that Putin was insulated from bad news.

Solovyov asked on air why Bonya had not yet been designated "a foreign agent" - a label with negative Soviet-era spying connotations - and asked Russia's top state investigator to check if her outburst had broken any laws.

"It's not ‚Äåup to this worn-out harlot to open her dirty mouth and clog up ‚Äãthe information space," said Solovyov.

Bonya hit back, vowing to use petitions and a potential lawsuit ‚Äãto have Solovyov taken off air.

Attempting to turn the tables, ‚Äãshe also asked authorities to check whether the sometimes extreme language he uses in his broadcasts about various people ‚Äåand groups broke Russian law.

A jokey new video, created with ‚Äãthe help of artificial intelligence and ‚Äãviewed 10 million times in just 24 hours, showed Bonya dressed in a Spider-Man outfit firing a web at Solovyov's face and taking on two other outspoken male public figures she accuses of misogyny.

On Moscow's streets, passers-by interviewed by Reuters mostly sympathised with Bonya.

Nadezhda, a ‚Äãmanager, said she believed any Russian - even if ‚Äåthey were outside Russia - had the right to talk about their country.

"I didn't like it that they started to insult her," ‚Äãshe said. "It seems absolutely wrong from people who have some kind of influence in society."

title: Putin finally admits Russia‚Äôs economy is in trouble and grasps for answers,¬Ýafter warnings about a financial crisis have been piling up
description: Putin revealed that GDP shrank by combined 1.8% in January and February, adding that manufacturing, industrial production and construction were negative.
fullText:
Russian President Vladimir Putin made his concerns about the economy public as he vented frustration at aides and demanded they come up with solutions.

During a televised meeting on the economy Wednesday, he revealed that GDP shrank by combined 1.8% in January and February, adding that manufacturing, industrial production and construction were negative.

What financial crisis risks is Russia currently facing?

How are Western sanctions impacting Russia's oil revenues?

How is Russia's labor shortage affecting economic growth?

Why is Russia's economy contracting for first time since 2022?

‚ÄúI expect to hear detailed reports today on the current economic situation and why the trajectory of macroeconomic indicators is currently below expectations,‚Äù Putin said. ‚ÄúMoreover, below the expectations of not only experts and analysts, but also the forecasts of the government itself and the central bank of Russia.‚Äù

The meeting was attended by Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin, Kremlin Deputy Chief of Staff Maxim Oreshkin, First Deputy Prime Minister Denis Manturov, Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak, Central Bank Governor Elvira Nabiullina, and ‚Äãthe CEO of PSB ‚Äãbank.

Russia‚Äôs economy had already been slowing down as Putin‚Äôs war on Ukraine continues to keep inflation high and the labor market tight.

An economic contraction would be the first since 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine and was hit by Western sanctions that slashed energy exports.

Massive military spending helped GDP expand by 4.1% in 2023 and 4.9% in 2024. But weak oil revenue and deeper deficits forced Moscow to limit defense outlays. GDP grew by just 1% last year, and the Kremlin earlier predicted 1.3% growth this year.

Meanwhile, the Kremlin‚Äôs budget deficit widened to $58.6 billion in the first quarter as oil tax revenue in March dropped by half compared to a year ago.

To be sure, the Iran war sent oil prices soaring, and the Trump administration has lifted sanctions on Russian oil, setting up Moscow for a revenue windfall. But Ukraine‚Äôs relentless drone attacks on Russian export hubs have prevented Russia from fully capitalizing on its opportunity.

Following Putin‚Äôs scolding of his aides on Wednesday, the central bank chief said Thursday that Russia‚Äôs unemployment rate remained at a historic low of 2% as the war created a lack of available workers, forcing employers to compete for staff.

‚ÄúThe peculiarity of the current situation is that for the first time in modern history, our economy has ‚Äåfaced shortages or limits on labor,‚Äù Nabiullina added. ‚ÄúThis is a new reality for the government and for business alike. In the past, high-rate cycles were tied to temporary external shocks, and once things stabilized, we cut rates fairly quickly. Now, however, we are facing a persistent downturn in external conditions affecting both exports and imports.‚Äù
